meta.title: Global Data Breach Tracker
meta.description: A curated record of major breaches of personally identifiable information (PII) worldwide. Record counts and attributions reflect the most widely reported figures at time of disclosure and may be revised.

breaches.name: Yahoo
breaches.attacker: Suspected state-sponsored actors; in 2017 the U.S. indicted two Russian FSB officers and two hackers in connection with related 2014 intrusion
breaches.method: Forged cookies and stolen backup copies of the user database
breaches.summary: Yahoo suffered the largest known data breach in history. An August 2013 intrusion was ultimately found to affect all 3 billion user accounts. A separate 2014 breach affected 500 million accounts.
breaches.aftermath: The disclosure during Verizon's acquisition of Yahoo reduced the sale price by about $350 million. Yahoo paid a $35 million SEC penalty and settled consumer litigation for $117.5 million.

breaches.name: Aadhaar (UIDAI)
breaches.attacker: Unknown; access reportedly sold via anonymous sellers on messaging apps
breaches.method: Unauthorized access and resale of admin credentials to the national identity database
breaches.summary: Reporters demonstrated that access to India's Aadhaar database — covering more than a billion residents — could be purchased for around 500 rupees, exposing the personal records of nearly the entire population.
breaches.aftermath: UIDAI denied a breach of biometric data but introduced 'virtual ID' and limited-access measures. The incident fueled an ongoing national debate over privacy and the Supreme Court's right-to-privacy ruling.

breaches.name: National Public Data
breaches.attacker: Threat actor 'USDoD'; data later leaked free on hacking forums
breaches.method: Unauthorized access to background-check data broker; database scraped and posted for sale
breaches.summary: A background-check company exposed a database containing billions of records, including an estimated 270 million Social Security numbers compiled on U.S., U.K. and Canadian citizens.
breaches.aftermath: Parent company Jerico Pictures filed for bankruptcy amid a wave of lawsuits. The breach is considered one of the largest exposures of Social Security numbers ever.

breaches.name: Facebook
breaches.attacker: Unknown scrapers; data leaked free on a hacking forum in 2021
breaches.method: Abuse of a contact-importer feature to scrape profile data at scale (patched 2019)
breaches.summary: Personal data of 533 million Facebook users across 106 countries was posted for free online in 2021, originating from a 2019 vulnerability that allowed mass scraping of phone numbers tied to accounts.
breaches.aftermath: Ireland's Data Protection Commission fined Meta €265 million under GDPR for failing to protect user data.

breaches.name: Marriott / Starwood
breaches.attacker: Suspected Chinese state-sponsored intelligence operation
breaches.method: Long-term undetected intrusion into the Starwood reservation system, present before Marriott's 2016 acquisition
breaches.summary: Marriott disclosed that its Starwood guest reservation database had been compromised since 2014, exposing up to 383 million guest records including millions of passport numbers.
breaches.aftermath: The U.K. ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million. The breach is widely attributed to Chinese intelligence-gathering operations.

breaches.name: Equifax
breaches.attacker: Four members of China's People's Liberation Army (PLA), indicted by the U.S. in 2020
breaches.method: Exploitation of an unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability (CVE-2017-5638)
breaches.summary: Attackers exploited a known, unpatched web-application flaw to access the credit-reporting files of 147 million Americans — one of the most damaging exposures of financial PII ever.
breaches.aftermath: Equifax agreed to a settlement of up to $700 million with the FTC, CFPB and U.S. states. The CEO resigned and the U.S. indicted four PLA members.

breaches.name: Anthem
breaches.attacker: Suspected Chinese state-sponsored actors
breaches.method: Spear-phishing leading to stolen administrator credentials
breaches.summary: Health insurer Anthem disclosed that attackers had accessed a database containing the records of nearly 79 million current and former members and employees.
breaches.aftermath: Anthem reached a record $115 million class-action settlement and a $16 million HIPAA settlement with HHS — the largest of its kind at the time.

breaches.name: Capital One
breaches.attacker: Paige Thompson, a former AWS engineer (convicted 2022)
breaches.method: Exploitation of a misconfigured web application firewall to access data stored in AWS S3
breaches.summary: A former cloud engineer exploited a misconfigured firewall to access the credit-card application data of about 106 million people in the U.S. and Canada.
breaches.aftermath: Capital One paid an $80 million regulatory fine and $190 million to settle consumer claims. Thompson was convicted of wire fraud and computer crimes.

breaches.name: U.S. Office of Personnel Management
breaches.attacker: Suspected Chinese state-sponsored actors
breaches.method: Stolen credentials and undetected long-term network intrusion
breaches.summary: The U.S. government agency that manages federal employee records disclosed that background-investigation data — including 5.6 million fingerprints — on 21.5 million current, former and prospective employees was stolen.
breaches.aftermath: The OPM director resigned. The breach is regarded as one of the most damaging compromises of U.S. national-security personnel data.

breaches.name: Target
breaches.attacker: Eastern European cybercriminals (point-of-sale malware operators)
breaches.method: Network access via stolen HVAC-vendor credentials, then POS memory-scraping malware
breaches.summary: During the 2013 holiday season, attackers stole 40 million payment-card records and personal data of up to 70 million customers from Target's point-of-sale systems.
breaches.aftermath: Target paid an $18.5 million multistate settlement and tens of millions more to banks and card networks. The CEO and CIO departed.

breaches.name: Uber
breaches.attacker: Two hackers who extorted the company
breaches.method: Stolen AWS credentials found in a private GitHub repository
breaches.summary: Attackers accessed data on 57 million riders and drivers. Uber paid the hackers $100,000 to delete the data and stay quiet, concealing the breach for over a year.
breaches.aftermath: Uber paid $148 million to settle with U.S. states for the cover-up. Its former security chief was later convicted of obstruction for concealing the breach.

breaches.name: T-Mobile
breaches.attacker: John Binns, a 21-year-old American hacker who claimed responsibility
breaches.method: Exploitation of an exposed, unprotected router / gateway into the network
breaches.summary: T-Mobile confirmed the theft of personal data on more than 76 million current, former and prospective customers, including millions of Social Security and driver's license numbers.
breaches.aftermath: T-Mobile agreed to a $350 million class-action settlement and pledged $150 million in security upgrades. It was one of several repeat breaches at the carrier.

breaches.name: 23andMe
breaches.attacker: Threat actor 'Golem' who advertised data, including lists targeting Ashkenazi Jewish and Chinese users
breaches.method: Credential-stuffing using passwords reused from prior breaches, amplified via the DNA Relatives feature
breaches.summary: Attackers used recycled passwords to access about 14,000 accounts, then leveraged the relatives-matching feature to scrape data on roughly 6.9 million users.
breaches.aftermath: 23andMe agreed to a $30 million settlement, faced regulatory action, and later filed for bankruptcy in 2025 amid declining trust.

breaches.name: Change Healthcare
breaches.attacker: ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware group
breaches.method: Compromised credentials on a Citrix remote-access portal lacking multi-factor authentication
breaches.summary: A ransomware attack on UnitedHealth's Change Healthcare paralyzed U.S. medical claims processing for weeks and exposed the health data of an estimated 100 million-plus people — the largest healthcare breach in U.S. history.
breaches.aftermath: UnitedHealth paid a reported $22 million ransom, took an estimated $2+ billion financial hit, and testified before Congress over the nationwide disruption to providers and pharmacies.

breaches.name: Ticketmaster (Snowflake)
breaches.attacker: ShinyHunters, via the broader Snowflake-customer campaign
breaches.method: Stolen credentials used to access a Snowflake cloud data warehouse lacking MFA
breaches.summary: Attackers offered for sale a database of up to 560 million Ticketmaster customers, part of a wider campaign that hit many companies using the Snowflake cloud platform.
breaches.aftermath: Part of the Snowflake-linked wave of 2024 breaches; led to arrests of alleged perpetrators and an industry push toward mandatory MFA.

breaches.name: AT&T (Snowflake)
breaches.attacker: Threat actors in the Snowflake-customer campaign (linked to ShinyHunters)
breaches.method: Stolen Snowflake credentials without MFA
breaches.summary: AT&T disclosed that records of calls and texts for nearly all of its ~110 million wireless customers over several months in 2022 were stolen from a third-party cloud platform.
breaches.aftermath: AT&T reportedly paid a ransom to have the stolen data deleted. The breach intensified scrutiny of Snowflake-customer security practices.

breaches.name: MOVEit / Clop campaign
breaches.attacker: Clop (Cl0p) ransomware group
breaches.method: Zero-day SQL injection vulnerability in Progress Software's MOVEit Transfer (CVE-2023-34362)
breaches.summary: Clop exploited a zero-day in the widely used MOVEit file-transfer tool to steal data from over 2,700 organizations and an estimated 95 million-plus individuals worldwide, including government agencies and universities.
breaches.aftermath: One of the largest supply-chain breaches ever, triggering thousands of downstream notifications and lawsuits across multiple countries.

breaches.name: LinkedIn (scraping)
breaches.attacker: Threat actor 'GOD User'; data advertised on a hacking forum
breaches.method: Mass scraping of public and API-exposed profile data
breaches.summary: Data scraped from 700 million LinkedIn profiles — about 90% of its users — was put up for sale, combining public profile fields with some non-public data such as phone numbers.
breaches.aftermath: LinkedIn maintained the data was scraped rather than breached, but the incident renewed debate over scraping and platform data protection.

breaches.name: Sony PlayStation Network
breaches.attacker: Unknown intruders (during a period of activist attention to Sony)
breaches.method: Network intrusion exploiting unpatched server vulnerabilities
breaches.summary: Sony took the PlayStation Network offline for 23 days after attackers compromised the accounts of 77 million users, in one of the largest gaming-related breaches.
breaches.aftermath: The U.K. ICO fined Sony £250,000. Sony offered 'Welcome Back' compensation and faced class-action litigation.

breaches.name: Adobe
breaches.attacker: Unknown
breaches.method: Network intrusion accessing customer databases and product source code
breaches.summary: Adobe initially reported 2.9 million affected accounts, later revised to about 153 million records, including poorly encrypted passwords and password hints.
breaches.aftermath: Adobe settled consumer claims and paid state penalties. The weakly protected password data became a widely studied example of poor cryptographic practice.

breaches.name: Ashley Madison
breaches.attacker: Group calling itself 'The Impact Team'
breaches.method: Network intrusion followed by public extortion and data dump
breaches.summary: Hackers stole and published the full customer database of the infidelity-focused dating site, exposing the identities of about 32 million users worldwide.
breaches.aftermath: The leak was linked to extortion attempts, divorces and reported suicides. Parent company paid a $1.6 million FTC settlement and restructured.

breaches.name: PowerSchool
breaches.attacker: A threat actor who extorted the company; a suspect was later charged in the U.S.
breaches.method: Stolen credentials of a support contractor used to access a customer support portal
breaches.summary: The K-12 education software provider disclosed a breach exposing data on more than 62 million students and roughly 10 million teachers across U.S. and Canadian school districts.
breaches.aftermath: PowerSchool paid a ransom to suppress the data, but schools later faced follow-on extortion. A suspect was arrested and charged in 2025.

breaches.name: AT&T (2025 dark-web leak)
breaches.attacker: Unknown sellers; data tied to prior 2024 intrusions
breaches.method: Re-compilation and posting of data linked to earlier AT&T breaches
breaches.summary: A dataset with personal details of more than 86 million AT&T customers — including roughly 44 million Social Security numbers — appeared for sale on the dark web in 2025.
breaches.aftermath: Renewed scrutiny of AT&T's repeated exposures and questions about whether the data came from previously disclosed incidents.

breaches.name: Qantas
breaches.attacker: Scattered Spider (a financially motivated cybercrime collective)
breaches.method: Social-engineering of a third-party call-center platform
breaches.summary: Australia's flagship airline disclosed a breach affecting about 5.7 million unique customer records, attributed to the Scattered Spider group's targeting of the aviation sector.
breaches.aftermath: Qantas obtained a court injunction to block release of the stolen data and faced regulatory scrutiny under Australian privacy law.

breaches.name: Prosper Marketplace
breaches.attacker: Unknown
breaches.method: Compromised administrative credentials with excessive database permissions
breaches.summary: The peer-to-peer lending platform exposed about 17.6 million records of sensitive PII, one of the largest single-incident exposures of 2025 by record count.
breaches.aftermath: Prosper notified affected users and offered credit monitoring amid regulatory review.

breaches.name: Yale New Haven Health
breaches.attacker: Unknown
breaches.method: Exploitation of a third-party file-transfer service vulnerability
breaches.summary: Connecticut's largest health system disclosed a breach affecting more than 5.5 million patients, traced to a vulnerability in a third-party data-transfer tool.
breaches.aftermath: The health system offered credit monitoring and faced class-action litigation.

breaches.name: Orange Group
breaches.attacker: Hacker known as 'Rey'
breaches.method: Intrusion into internal systems after a failed ransom demand, with data leaked publicly
breaches.summary: A hacker leaked more than 600,000 records and several gigabytes of internal data from Orange Romania after the telecom giant refused a ransom.
breaches.aftermath: Orange confirmed a breach of a non-critical application and launched an investigation.

breaches.name: CarGurus
breaches.attacker: Unknown
breaches.method: Unauthorized access to the marketplace platform's user database
breaches.summary: The automotive marketplace disclosed a breach reportedly affecting more than 12 million registered users in early 2026.
breaches.aftermath: Investigation ongoing; affected users notified and prompted to reset credentials.

breaches.name: Odido
breaches.attacker: Unknown
breaches.method: Unauthorized access discovered during a security investigation
breaches.summary: The Dutch telecom operator disclosed a cyberattack that may have affected up to 6.2 million customers after investigators found unauthorized access to its systems.
breaches.aftermath: Odido notified customers and Dutch data-protection authorities; investigation ongoing.

breaches.name: Medtronic
breaches.attacker: ShinyHunters (claimed)
breaches.method: Unauthorized third-party access to certain corporate IT systems
breaches.summary: The medical-device maker disclosed unauthorized access to corporate IT systems; the ShinyHunters group claimed theft of more than 9 million records and set a ransom deadline.
breaches.aftermath: Investigation ongoing as of mid-2026; Medtronic engaged authorities and external responders.

breaches.name: Carnival Cruise Line
breaches.attacker: ShinyHunters (claimed)
breaches.method: Unauthorized access to customer and loyalty-program systems
breaches.summary: Carnival notified roughly 6 million individuals after a breach; ShinyHunters claimed 8.7 million records, with analysis indicating around 7.5 million Mariner Society loyalty accounts affected.
breaches.aftermath: Carnival notified regulators and affected guests; investigation ongoing in 2026.

breaches.name: Instructure / Canvas (education)
breaches.attacker: Unknown criminals who advertised institution-level datasets
breaches.method: Compromise affecting education platforms used by thousands of institutions
breaches.summary: Criminals circulated a list of 8,809 school districts, universities and online education platforms with record counts ranging from tens of thousands to several million per institution.
breaches.aftermath: Affected institutions began notifications; scope still being assessed in 2026.

breaches.name: MGM Resorts
breaches.attacker: Scattered Spider with ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware
breaches.method: Social-engineering of the IT help desk to reset credentials (vishing)
breaches.summary: A help-desk social-engineering attack crippled MGM's casino and hotel operations for days and exposed personal data of about 37 million prior guests.
breaches.aftermath: MGM reported roughly $100 million in losses. The attack became a landmark case study in social-engineering risk.

breaches.name: CAM4
breaches.attacker: No attacker; exposed by misconfiguration
breaches.method: Misconfigured Elasticsearch server left publicly accessible without authentication
breaches.summary: Security researchers found an unsecured database belonging to the adult streaming site CAM4 exposing nearly 11 billion records — one of the largest exposures ever recorded by row count.
breaches.aftermath: The server was secured after disclosure. No confirmed malicious access, but the volume of sensitive data made it a notable privacy incident.
